![Image 1: Loading video](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/1zJWirDS63o/sddefault.jpg#404_is_fine)
AI Decoded speaks to leading tech podcaster Scott Galloway about the growing controversy surrounding Anthropic's latest AI model, Claude Mythos.

During testing, the system uncovered thousands of hidden vulnerabilities in the software underpinning most of the world's computers — prompting Anthropic to decide it was too dangerous to release.

The company says Mythos can outperform humans at certain hacking and cyber‑security tasks, raising fresh concerns among regulators, lawmakers and financial institutions about the risks to global digital infrastructure.

Also on the show, we reveal how US students are changing their majors at university in order to 'AI proof' their future careers and how nearly half of Gen Z would like to turn back the clock to the late 90's before AI was even a thing.

Joining Christian Fraser is the BBC's AI correspondent Marc Cieslak and AI Decoded co-host Priya Lakhani.

Photo: cdn.theatlantic.com[· rights & takedowns](https://letsdatascience.com/copyright)

Quick Summary Hide

The Atlantic reports that rising adoption of AI coding agents, led by Anthropic's Claude Code, has shifted industry concerns from an "AI bubble" to rapid commercial revenue growth. The Atlantic writes that **Anthropic**'s revenue is increasing faster than Zoom during the pandemic, Google in the early 2000s, and Standard Oil, and that, if the current growth rate continued, the company would out-earn every firm by early next year. The Atlantic attributes the step change to the November release of Claude Code, which the article says can run autonomous agents that complete programming tasks with minimal human edits. The Atlantic also notes comparable advances in Codex and Cursor, and quotes Ethan Mollick calling the update "a step change."

Image

![Image 1](https://static01.nyt.com/images/2020/04/21/multimedia/THE-MORNING-SECTION-BREAK/THE-MORNING-SECTION-BREAK-articleLarge.jpg?quality=75&auto=webp&disable=upscale)

Image

![Image 2: Sam Altman of OpenAI, in a dark suit and blue shirt and tie, seen through a courthouse window.](https://static01.nyt.com/images/2026/05/01/multimedia/01db-altman-oai-fcbj/01db-altman-oai-fcbj-articleLarge.jpg?quality=75&auto=webp&disable=upscale)

Sam Altman, right, has suggested that OpenAI, when releasing its latest A.I. model, would take a different approach than its rival, Anthropic, did. Credit...Brennan Smart for The New York Times

**Andrew here.** The [conversation we began](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/30/business/dealbook/powell-fed-trump-warsh.html) on Thursday about Anthropic’s potential computing constraints is continuing, as OpenAI unveils a new model that supporters say doesn’t suffer from a lack of resources. We look at what that might mean for businesses, customers and national security.

Also, Apple blew away earnings expectations. But as it faces questions about its A.I. strategy, the company announced a new plan that could give it more cash to invest in the technology. More below.

## The compute question

Earlier this week, many in the technology industry were asking whether OpenAI was in danger of falling behind in the artificial intelligence race. But the release of the company’s new model may change its fortunes — and is contributing to a wider debate about the importance of computing resources.

**Meet GPT-5.5-Cyber.** The mouthful of a name is [OpenAI’s newest cybersecurity model](https://x.com/sama/status/2049712078836170843), which Sam Altman, the company’s C.E.O., said on Thursday would be released to “critical cyber defenders” in a few days.

Altman said OpenAI would work “with the entire ecosystem and the government” to figure out how to quickly roll out wider access for the model in the name of safeguarding global computing systems. “We want to rapidly help secure companies/infrastructure,” he added.

**It was an implicit rebuke to his rival Anthropic,** even as Altman is [doing something similar](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/30/after-dissing-anthropic-for-limiting-mythos-openai-restricts-access-to-cyber-too/). Anthropic has restricted access to its Claude Mythos Preview model to several dozen companies and a growing number of U.S. federal agencies, arguing that Mythos’s ability to ferret out cybersecurity threats was a potential weapon that could be easily abused.

The last two quarters have proven that Google’s cloud business is a real player, growing faster than Microsoft’s Azure and Amazon’s AWS.  (Thomas Lohnes/Getty Images)

The rules have changed for Big Tech earnings season. Beating expectations and providing good guidance isn’t enough anymore, and it’s not entirely clear what the mark for enough is. During Wednesday afternoon’s lightning round of[Microsoft](https://www.barrons.com/market-data/stocks/msft?mod=article_chiclet),[Alphabet](https://www.barrons.com/market-data/stocks/googl?mod=article_chiclet),[Amazon.com](https://www.barrons.com/market-data/stocks/amzn?mod=article_chiclet), and[Meta Platforms](https://www.barrons.com/market-data/stocks/meta?mod=article_chiclet)reporting earnings all at once, the reactions were mixed and at times chaotic.

https://cleantechnica.com/2026/05/01/tes.title: Tesla Got $573 Million from SpaceX and xAI in 2025
https://cleantechnica.com/2026/05/01/tes.text:
URL Source: https://cleantechnica.com/2026/05/01/tesla-got-573-million-from-spacex-and-xai-in-2025/

Published Time: 2026-05-02T03:53:18+00:00

Markdown Content:
![Image 1](https://cleantechnica.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Tesla-Battery-4680-SpaceX-Rocket-CleanTechnica-Watermark-800x445.jpeg)

* * *

**_Support CleanTechnica's work through [a Substack subscription](https://cleantechnica.substack.com/subscribe) or [on Stripe](https://cleantechnica.fundjournalism.org/contribute/)._**

* * *

There’s been a lot of talk lately that Tesla may well end up merging with SpaceX, and with both companies shifting more and more toward AI and its supportive infrastructure, perhaps that is now Elon Musk’s big plan. Of course, both Tesla and SpaceX have built their fortunes on other things. Tesla builds cars and deploys batteries, and SpaceX builds rockets and launches satellites. What has been a bit surprising lately, though, is just how many cars and batteries Tesla is selling to SpaceX and xAI….

[A couple of weeks ago](https://cleantechnica.com/2026/04/16/spacex-dominating-tesla-cybertruck-sales/), I reported on the fact that “18% of Cybertruck registrations in the 4th quarter — 1,279 of them — were for SpaceX.” That added up to more than $100 million in sales. However, that wasn’t even close to the total amount of money SpaceX and xAI (now part of SpaceX) spent on Tesla vehicles last year.

Discover more

Home EV Charging

Energy Storage Systems

Microgrids Explained Guide

“Tesla Inc. generated more than half a billion dollars in revenue last year from selling products to two of Elon Musk’s other companies, the carmaker disclosed in an amended annual filing,” [_Bloomberg_](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-30/tesla-raked-573-million-in-sales-from-spacex-and-xai-last-year%3Fsrnd%3Dhomepage-americas?embedded-checkout=true) reports. “The bulk of the sales — about $430.1 million — came from doing business with xAI, Musk’s artificial intelligence startup, Tesla first[disclosed](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1318605/000162828026003952/tsla-20251231.htm "Tesla's initial 10-K")in January. In an[amended filing](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1318605/000110465926053166/tm2611837d1_10ka.htm "Tesla 10-K/A") uploaded Thursday, the automaker added that it also recognized another $143.3 million in revenue from the billionaire’s closely held rocket manufacturer SpaceX.”

xAI has been buying Tesla Megapacks (giant batteries). So, while many have been cheering on Tesla’s battery storage sales growth, keep in mind that $430.1 million of that in 2025 was from another Musk company. Meanwhile, Tesla has been integrating Grok (xAI’s chatbot) into its vehicles.

## Tech Stocks

## With Apple officially retiring its net-cash-neutral target, some suspect the company will look to conduct more transformative M&A, beyond the smaller deals it made in the past

Last Updated: May 1, 2026 at 10:31 p.m. ET

First Published: May 1, 2026 at 3:35 p.m. ET

Add us on Google

Choose MarketWatch as a preferred source of financial news

Apple’s next CEO, John Ternus, isn’t sharing his strategy just yet — but the company just made one notable change before he’s even started on the job.

As the iPhone maker prepares to launch its long-awaited artificial-intelligence features later this year, a sudden shift in its financial strategy suggests the company may end up making a big purchase soon.

Apple isn’t exactly known for being the first to jump on a new technology or open its wallet in pursuit of flashy acquisitions. While other Big Tech peers have raced to spend hundreds of billions of dollars on capital expenditures, Apple has been on the sidelines — returning money to shareholders through stock buybacks and dividends.

The company could get in the game soon, however. On its second-quarter earnings call Thursday, [Apple shared](https://www.marketwatch.com/livecoverage/apple-earnings-stock-results-iphone-ai-aapl) that it would be abandoning its longstanding target of achieving a net-cash-neutral position.

Back in 2018, the company started winnowing down its massive cash pile with the goal of maintaining equivalent cash and debt levels, but Apple said it will evaluate the two metrics separately going forward. The decision will help it “make more optimal economic decisions around how we best utilize our debt and cash portfolios to support the business,” CFO Kevan Parekh said.

“We believe the changing of the guard at Apple with a new CEO hints at acquisitions going forward, which is a relief for investors to hear,” Wedbush analyst Dan Ives told MarketWatch over email.

Europe should be known for BottleCap AI, not [bottle cap memes](https://www.reddit.com/r/ExplainTheJoke/comments/1gllkyw/not_european_whats_up_with_the_bottle/). With its tongue-in-cheek name, this Prague-based AI startup is one of the teams that VCs think you should know.

It is not that European startups never cut through the noise — [Lovable](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/28/lovable-launches-its-vibe-coding-app-on-ios-and-android/) and [Mistral AI](https://techcrunch.com/2025/09/09/what-is-mistral-ai-everything-to-know-about-the-openai-competitor/) are proof of it. But there are many more that don’t have [nine digits in annual recurring revenue](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/11/lovable-says-it-added-100m-in-revenue-last-month-alone-with-just-146-employees/) yet and that insiders are still tracking very closely.

That’s where this list comes in. Over the last few weeks, we asked investors at some of Europe’s best known venture funds to recommend two startups each: one from their portfolio (because they liked the startup well enough to invest) and one outside of it (because they are the startup experts but can’t invest in them all). We also threw in a few picks of our own.

From pre-launch to [unicorn](https://techcrunch.com/2026/01/31/meet-the-new-european-unicorns-of-2026/), these startups are at different stages in their journey, and from different sectors. Due to our methodology, they may not reflect where the region’s hottest hubs are, but they do reflect how [deep tech talent](https://techcrunch.com/2025/12/30/76-european-deep-tech-university-spinouts-reached-unicorn-or-centaur-status/) could help Europe play its own cards in the AI race.

## Alta Ares

**_Recommended by Julien Codorniou, general partner, 20VC_.**

**What it does:**[Alta Ares](https://www.altaares.com/) develops AI-powered counter-drone systems.

**Why it’s worth watching:**Defense tech has gone from pariah to trending, particularly in Europe, where the war in Ukraine was a wake-up call for armies to modernize. Alta Ares’ interceptors answer a need for cheaper solutions to detect and fight drone incursions.

Techcrunch event

San Francisco, CA|October 13-15, 2026

## Apron

**_Recommended by Jan Hammer, partner, Index Ventures (investor)_.**

**What it does:**[Apron](https://getapron.com/) provides [invoice management for small business owners](https://techcrunch.com/2023/09/28/apron-grabs-15-million-to-simplify-invoice-processing/).

**Why it’s worth watching:**SMBs can be a lucrative segment for fintech companies; business owners are willing to spend at least some money to save time, and there are millions of them.

## Botify

**_Recommended by Claire Houry, general partner, Ventech (investor)_.**

**What it does:**[Botify](https://www.botify.com/) helps brands increase their visibility in AI searches.
